scenario: Apex Bank models a fraud-review embedded subprocess in Camunda 8.8 that groups 5 tasks inside a parent process. The team must respect the start event rules of the embedded subprocess to keep the model deployable on the engine.
question: Which start event constraint applies to an embedded subprocess?

options.text: One message start event is required and additional none start events may be added for alternative entry points to the subprocess
Exactly one none start event is required and other start event types are not allowed inside the embedded subprocess at all
One timer start event marks the subprocess entry and a none start event can be added when the subprocess is invoked manually
One signal start event activates the subprocess and a none start event is required only when boundary events are attached to it

a.text: Incorrect. An embedded subprocess does not accept a message start event; the only valid start event inside it is a single none start event.

b.text: Correct. An embedded subprocess must have exactly one none start event at its entry, and other start event types are not allowed inside it.

c.text: Incorrect. Timer start events are not permitted inside an embedded subprocess; the only allowed start event is a single none start event.

d.text: Incorrect. Signal start events are not permitted inside an embedded subprocess; the only allowed start event is a single none start event, regardless of boundary events.

explanation: An embedded subprocess must have exactly one none start event at its entry. Other start event types such as message, timer, or signal are not allowed in the subprocess body, which keeps the activation semantics aligned with the parent scope.
